problem: Sluggish starter that won't double
symptoms: An established starter rises slowly, peaks at less than 2x its original volume, or takes more than 10-12 hours to peak after feeding even though it shows some bubbles.
causes: Ambient temperature below 70F/21C, which roughly halves activity for every 10F drop
Feeding ratio too low (e.g., 1:1:1) so the starter goes acidic before it can build yeast population
Flour lacks nutrients (bleached or all-purpose only) and starves the wild yeast and LAB
Chlorinated tap water inhibiting microbial growth
fixes: Move starter to a warm spot (75-82F/24-28C); use a proofing box, the oven with the light on, or top of the fridge
Switch to a higher feeding ratio of 1:5:5 (e.g., 10g starter + 50g flour + 50g water) and feed every 12 hours for 3-5 days
Mix in 10-20% whole grain rye or whole wheat flour by weight to boost enzymatic activity and nutrients
Use filtered or dechlorinated water at 80-90F/27-32C when feeding
Track peak time across 3 feedings; expect doubling in 4-8 hours once revived

problem: Starter smells like acetone or nail polish remover
symptoms: Sharp solvent-like aroma, often accompanied by a layer of dark hooch and a deflated, flat starter that has clearly peaked and fallen.
causes: Starter is starving: yeast and bacteria exhausted available sugars and started metabolizing ethanol into ethyl acetate
Feeding ratio too low for the ambient temperature (1:1:1 in a warm kitchen burns through food too fast)
Too long between feedings, especially at room temperature
fixes: Feed sooner and more generously: switch to 1:5:5 or 1:10:10 ratio for 2-3 feedings
Stir hooch back in (adds tang) or pour off (milder flavor) before refeeding
If kept on the counter, feed every 12 hours; if you can't keep that schedule, refrigerate after peak and feed weekly
Smell normalizes to yogurt/beer aroma within 1-2 well-fed cycles; don't bake until it does

problem: Hooch (gray/brown liquid) on top of starter
symptoms: A thin layer of clear, gray, brown, or near-black liquid pools on top of an otherwise normal-looking starter, often with a strong alcoholic smell.
causes: Starter is hungry and fermentation produced excess ethanol that separated out
Hydration too high relative to flour mass for current feeding schedule
Long gap between feedings (especially refrigerator storage > 1-2 weeks)
fixes: Stir hooch back in for tangier flavor or pour off for milder flavor; either is safe (it is not mold)
Increase feeding ratio from 1:1:1 to 1:2:2 or 1:3:3 to extend time between meals
Reduce starter hydration toward 100% (equal weights flour and water) if currently running wetter
If from fridge: do 2-3 room-temperature feedings at 1:5:5 before using in a bake to fully restore activity

problem: Pink or orange streaks on starter (Serratia marcescens)
symptoms: Bright pink, red, or orange irregular streaks, spots, or film on the surface or through the starter, often with a foul, putrid smell distinct from normal sour aromas.
causes: Contamination by Serratia marcescens or other harmful bacteria, typically from a neglected, underfed starter
Warm, moist environment with infrequent feedings allowing pathogenic bacteria to outcompete LAB
Cross-contamination from dirty utensils or jars
fixes: Discard the entire starter immediately; do not bake with it or attempt to salvage it (Serratia can cause illness)
Wash and sanitize the jar, lid, and all utensils with hot soapy water, then dry completely
Start a new starter from scratch or from reliable backup discard
Do not confuse with the faint pinkish tinge sometimes seen with rye flour, which is uniform and odorless
Going forward: feed on schedule, store in fridge between bakes, and keep equipment clean

problem: Mold (fuzzy spots) on starter
symptoms: Fuzzy or hairy patches on the starter surface in any color: white, green, blue, black, or yellow. Distinct from smooth hooch or rye-induced discoloration.
causes: Long neglect with food sources still present (mold colonizes faster than the starter can re-acidify)
Contaminated jar, utensil, or flour
Lid left open allowing airborne spores to settle, especially in humid environments
fixes: Discard the entire starter; mold hyphae thread through the whole culture even when only the surface looks fuzzy
Sterilize the jar with boiling water or run it through a dishwasher cycle
Start fresh: typical timeline is 7-14 days to a usable starter, 4-8 weeks to full maturity
Prevent recurrence by feeding on schedule, covering with a cloth or loose lid (not airtight), and not letting the starter sit unfed > 5-7 days at room temp

problem: New starter rises dramatically on day 2-3 then dies
symptoms: A 1-3 day old starter triples in volume, looks foamy and very active, smells sweet/funky/cabbage-like (not sour), then goes flat and shows no activity for several days afterward.
causes: Leuconostoc and other early-stage bacteria producing CO2 before wild yeast establishes; this is a 'false rise', not real leavening
These bacteria thrive in the early non-acidic environment but die off as pH drops
fixes: Do NOT bake with a starter that has only shown a false rise; it has no real yeast power yet
Keep feeding 1:1:1 twice a day (every 12 hours) at 75-85F/24-29C with whole grain or whole grain blend flour
Expect a quiet 'dead zone' around days 4-7 as Leuconostoc dies off and wild yeast colonizes
Once the starter starts rising again predictably, gradually shift to 1:2:2, then 1:5:5 for maintenance
Wait until starter doubles reliably within 4-8 hours over multiple consecutive feedings before baking (typically 2-4 weeks total)

problem: Dough too sticky and slack to handle
symptoms: Dough sticks aggressively to hands and bench, will not hold a ball during shaping, spreads out flat in the bowl between folds, and never seems to develop a smooth surface.
causes: Hydration too high for the flour's protein/absorption capacity (common when copying recipes that use stronger flour)
Insufficient gluten development from too few or too gentle stretch and folds
Skipping or shortening autolyse, so flour has not fully hydrated
Over-fermentation: enzymes have begun degrading the gluten network
fixes: Reduce hydration by 5% (e.g., 78% -> 73%) on the next bake and reassess; many home bread flours top out around 72-75%
Add a 30-60 minute autolyse (flour + water only, rest before adding starter and salt)
Perform 4 sets of stretch-and-folds at 30-minute intervals during the first 2 hours of bulk; dough should noticeably tighten between sets
Use bread flour at 12-13% protein instead of all-purpose (~10-11%) for high-hydration formulas
If folds aren't tightening the dough by set 3-4, end bulk early; you're entering over-fermentation

problem: Dough too stiff and dry
symptoms: Dough resists stretching, tears instead of stretching during folds, feels like clay, and produces a dense crumb with poor oven spring.
causes: Hydration too low for the flour (whole grain and rye absorb significantly more water than white)
Dry kitchen environment pulling moisture from exposed dough
Inaccurate scaling: using volume measurements instead of weight
Flour switched to a higher-protein or whole-grain blend without adjusting water
fixes: Increase hydration by 3-5% next bake (e.g., 65% -> 70%) and observe handling
When using whole wheat or rye, add 5-10% extra water vs. an all-white formula at the same hydration
Always weigh flour and water in grams; cup measurements vary by 20%+
Use the bassinage technique: hold back 5-10% of the water and add it gradually after initial mix once you see how the flour absorbs
Cover dough with a tight-fitting lid or damp cloth between folds to prevent skin formation

problem: Dough tears during pre-shape or final shape
symptoms: The dough's outer skin rips when you try to round it, drag it, or build tension on the bench; you see exposed wet interior dough through tears in the surface.
causes: Over-tightening during pre-shape; gluten is already at maximum tension with no slack
No bench rest between pre-shape and final shape, so dough hasn't relaxed
Dough is too dry/stiff or over-developed from excessive folds/kneading
Bench too floured, creating friction that catches and tears the skin
fixes: Pre-shape gently: just enough to form a loose round with a barely-taut surface; stop the moment the skin tightens
Bench rest 20-40 minutes (up to 60 if still tight) between pre-shape and final shape
Use a lightly damp or bare bench with minimal flour; dust only the top of the dough lightly
If overworked: skip aggressive shaping moves, just do a simple letter fold and place seam-down in the banneton
Reduce stretch-and-fold sets from 6 to 3-4 if your dough is consistently tight at shaping

problem: Underproofed bulk fermentation
symptoms: Dough at end of bulk feels dense and tight, has barely risen (< 25-30%), shows few bubbles on the surface, and produces a dense loaf with a tight crumb after baking.
causes: Bulk ended too soon based on a recipe time rather than dough cues
Cool kitchen (below 70F/21C) slowing fermentation dramatically
Weak or not-yet-peaked starter at mix
Low inoculation (< 15% prefermented flour) without extending bulk time
fixes: Use an aliquot jar: take a small dough sample at end of mix, mark the level, and end bulk at 50-75% rise for tighter crumb or 75-100% for more open
Track dough temperature (DT); aim for 76-80F/24-27C and adjust water temperature to hit it
Rough bulk timing by DT: 80F ~5h, 75F ~7-8h, 70F ~12h, 65F ~16h
Mix only when starter has roughly doubled and passes the float test
If you suspect underproof, extend bulk by 1-2 hours and recheck; you can also do a longer warm final proof (1-2 hours) before retarding

problem: Overproofed bulk fermentation
symptoms: Dough is very loose and slack, has lost its dome and started to flatten or collapse at the edges of the bowl, smells strongly alcoholic or sour, and shows no spring back when poked.
causes: Bulk went too long for the temperature (frequent in warm kitchens or hot summers)
Starter was very young/active and fermented faster than expected
Too high inoculation (> 20-25% prefermented flour) without shortening bulk
Dough temperature drifted high (> 80F/27C) without timing adjustment
fixes: Skip the bench rest, shape immediately and gently with a single letter fold to preserve any remaining gas
Skip the room-temperature final proof and go straight into a cold retard at 36-39F/2-4C for 2-4 hours, then bake straight from the fridge
Score deeper than usual to compensate for reduced oven spring
Future bakes: end bulk earlier (target 50% rise instead of 75-100%), or lower DT by 3-5F by using cooler water
Consider reducing starter percentage by 5% next time (e.g., 20% -> 15%)

problem: Dough overproofs in the fridge during overnight cold retard
symptoms: Loaf looks great going into the fridge but emerges flat, slack, and gas-poor; spreads when turned out of the banneton; bakes with little oven spring and a dense crumb.
causes: Refrigerator running warmer than 40F/4C, allowing fermentation to continue actively
Bulk fermentation ended too late, so dough was already nearly fully proofed before going in
Too long a warm rest (> 1 hour) after shaping before refrigerating
Retard time too long for the starter strength and recipe (most loaves want 8-16 hours, not 24-48)
fixes: Verify fridge temperature with a thermometer; adjust to 36-39F/2-4C
End bulk fermentation 15-30 minutes earlier than usual when planning a long retard
After shaping, get the dough into the fridge within 30-45 minutes (cover loosely with a bag to prevent skin)
Cap retard at 12-16 hours for typical sourdough; if you need longer, end bulk at 30-40% rise instead of 50-75%
If already overproofed: bake cold straight from the fridge with deeper scoring

problem: Dough does not rise and bread has no oven spring
symptoms: Dough barely changes volume during bulk and final proof, comes out of the oven as a flat, dense puck with no rise, often with a smooth uncracked surface (no real opening at the score).
causes: Starter was not at peak activity when mixed (used too early or too late after peak)
Dough temperature too cold throughout bulk (< 65F/18C), effectively halting fermentation
Severely underproofed bulk; dough never built enough gas
Dutch oven or oven not preheated long enough (< 45 min) so initial blast of heat is too weak
Dead or contaminated starter (very rare if maintained)
fixes: Use starter at peak: it should have at least doubled, look domed and bubbly, and pass the float test
Target dough temperature of 76-78F/24-26C; use a heating pad or oven with light on for warmth
Preheat oven and Dutch oven to 500F/260C for a full 60 minutes before loading
Do a starter health check: feed 1:5:5, time the rise; if it doesn't double in 4-8 hours at 78F it needs revival before any bake
Bake bread at internal temperature 205-210F/96-99C; underbaked loaves will look unrisen even when they were fine

problem: Loaf spreads flat like a pancake when turned out
symptoms: Dough looks fine in the banneton but immediately flows outward into a wide, flat disc when tipped onto parchment or the peel, then bakes wide and short.
causes: Insufficient surface tension from shaping; dough has no taut skin to hold its form
Over-fermentation has degraded the gluten so it can't support the loaf's weight
Hydration too high for the flour and the baker's skill level
Banneton too large for the dough size, allowing the dough to relax sideways
fixes: Use the stitching method: after shaping in banneton, wait 10 minutes, then pinch and pull dough from one side to the other in a zigzag to add tension
Try double shaping: shape, rest 15 minutes, then re-shape with a tighter pull
Match banneton size to dough: a 750-900g loaf wants an 8-9 inch round or 10x4 inch oval
Drop hydration by 5% (e.g., 78% -> 73%) until you can shape consistently
Bake straight from a cold retard; cold dough holds shape much better than room-temp dough during transfer

problem: No ear, or blowouts on the side of the loaf
symptoms: The score doesn't open into a raised lip ('ear'); instead the loaf either stays sealed and bursts unpredictably along the side or base, or the score widens flat without lifting.
causes: Score too shallow (< 1/4 inch) so the dough seals over before it can expand
Blade held perpendicular (90 degrees) instead of angled, so no flap forms
Dough at room temperature when scored; too soft for clean cuts
Insufficient steam in the first 15-20 minutes prevents the crust from staying pliable
Overproofed dough has nothing left to spring, so the score can't lift
fixes: Score at a 30-45 degree angle, holding the lame nearly parallel to the surface, in a single confident stroke about 1/2 inch (1-1.2 cm) deep
Score directly out of the fridge; cold firm dough cuts cleanly and opens dramatically
Use one decisive arc rather than multiple sawing strokes
For a Dutch oven, keep the lid on for the first 20-25 minutes to trap steam
If blowouts persist along the base: deepen the main score and ensure dough is properly fermented (not over)

problem: Thick, tough, hard crust
symptoms: Crust is uncomfortably hard to bite, can be over 1/4 inch thick, sometimes shatters or hurts the gums, and stays hard even hours after cooling.
causes: Bake time too long, especially in the uncovered phase
Hydration too low (< 70%) producing less internal steam
Excess flour (especially rice flour) on the loaf surface from bannetons
Oven temperature too low for the bake duration, drying out crust slowly instead of setting it quickly
fixes: Shorten the uncovered bake: e.g., 20 min covered + 15-20 min uncovered at 450F/230C, instead of 30+ min uncovered
Increase hydration toward 75-78% to generate more steam
Brush off excess rice flour from the loaf surface before scoring
Bake hotter for a shorter total time (e.g., 475F/245C for 35-40 min total instead of 425F/220C for 50 min)
Wrap the hot loaf in a clean cotton tea towel as it cools to soften the crust slightly through trapped moisture

problem: Pale crust with no browning
symptoms: Loaf comes out blonde, tan, or pale yellow rather than golden-brown; crust looks anemic and lacks the flavor depth of a properly baked sourdough.
causes: Oven not hot enough; Maillard browning needs 280-330F/140-165C surface temperatures and reliable heat
Not enough time in the uncovered phase to drive surface browning
Overproofed dough: yeast and bacteria consumed nearly all residual sugars, leaving nothing for Maillard reactions
Oven thermostat reading high (actual temp lower than displayed)
Dough straight from a long retard with too much acidity slows browning
fixes: Verify oven temperature with a separate oven thermometer; recalibrate if needed
Preheat 60 minutes at 500F/260C before loading
After removing the lid, bake uncovered until deep golden-brown (15-25 min), not just by the clock
If consistently pale, bump set temperature up by 25F/15C for the uncovered phase
End bulk fermentation a touch earlier (preserves residual sugars) and shorten cold retards beyond 16 hours

problem: Crust burning before the crumb is cooked
symptoms: Outside is dark brown or black with a hard shell, but slicing reveals a gummy, dense, or visibly underbaked center; internal temperature below 200F/93C.
causes: Oven runs hot relative to thermostat
Bake temperature too high for the loaf size, especially for large or high-hydration loaves
Loaf positioned too close to the heating element (top or bottom)
Uncovered phase started too early and ran too long
fixes: Verify oven temperature with an external thermometer
After the initial 20 min covered at 475-500F/245-260C, drop to 425-450F/220-230C for the uncovered phase
If crust darkens too fast, tent the loaf loosely with foil for the last 10-15 minutes
Position the rack in the lower-middle of the oven and place a sheet pan on the rack below to deflect bottom heat
Always bake to internal temperature: 205-210F/96-99C measured in the center; do not rely on color or time alone

problem: Gummy, sticky crumb
symptoms: When sliced, the crumb feels wet, dense, and pasty; it sticks to the knife, balls up between fingers, and doesn't have the airy springiness of a properly baked loaf.
causes: Loaf cut before fully cooled (interior continues to cook and set during cooling)
Underbaked: internal temperature below 200F/93C when pulled
Underproofed: gluten structure too compact for moisture to escape during the bake
Severely overproofed: gluten collapsed and gas channels merged, trapping moisture
fixes: Cool completely on a wire rack for at least 1-2 hours before slicing; for high-hydration loaves wait 4 hours or more
Bake to an internal temperature of 205-210F/96-99C; insert thermometer through the bottom
Diagnose proofing: if crumb is uniformly dense -> underproofed; if collapsed and tunneled with wet spots -> overproofed
If chronically gummy at correct internal temp, extend uncovered bake by 5-10 minutes or drop oven by 25F and bake 10 minutes longer
Always weigh loaves; very large loaves (> 1kg dough) need 10-15 minutes additional bake time

problem: Dense, tight crumb (uniformly small holes)
symptoms: Crumb structure is uniformly compact with very small, evenly-spaced holes throughout; loaf feels heavy for its size and lacks the open, airy texture expected of artisan sourdough.
causes: Underfermented bulk: dough never built enough gas to create open structure
Weak or immature starter (a new starter needs 4-8 weeks to fully mature)
Cold dough temperature throughout bulk (< 70F/21C)
Over-handling at shaping that degassed the dough excessively
Hydration too low for the desired open crumb
fixes: Strengthen starter first: feed 1:5:5 twice daily until it consistently doubles in 4-6 hours and passes the float test
Extend bulk to a 75-100% rise (use an aliquot jar to measure)
Hit a dough temperature of 76-80F/24-27C using warm water (calculate: DT*3 - flour temp - room temp = water temp)
Shape gently with one letter fold and a light final shape; avoid degassing aggressively
Increase hydration by 3-5% if your flour can handle it (try 70% -> 73-75%)

problem: Fool's crumb (a few huge holes surrounded by dense areas)
symptoms: Cross-section shows one or two giant tunnels or caverns near the top, with the rest of the crumb tight, dense, or even gummy; looks 'open' at a glance but the structure is uneven and unsuitable for sandwiches.
causes: Underfermented bulk: gas pooled into a few weak spots instead of distributing evenly
Insufficient or rushed shaping that didn't pop large existing bubbles or build even tension
Overly stiff dough that trapped gas in pockets rather than distributing it
High-protein flour shaped without adequate degassing of large bubbles
fixes: Extend bulk fermentation; aim for 75-100% rise on an aliquot jar instead of stopping at 30-50%
During pre-shape and shape, gently press out any visibly large bubbles you can feel under the surface
Use a more thorough shaping pattern (letter fold + roll, or stitching) to even out gas distribution
Check starter vitality: a sluggish starter often produces fool's crumb because bulk had to be cut short
Reduce dough stiffness slightly with a 2-3% hydration bump if working with strong bread flour
